summary: ʿAdl is the doctrine of divine justice, the principle that God acts only justly and does no wrong. It is one of the five fundamental principles (al-usul al-khamsa) of the Mu'tazila and a defining commitment of their theology.
description: ʿAdl affirms that God is just and that injustice cannot be attributed to Him. For the Mu'tazila it is the second of their five principles (al-usul al-khamsa), after divine oneness (tawhid). From this principle the Mu'tazila drew far-reaching conclusions: because a just God cannot fairly punish acts He Himself compels, human beings must possess genuine causal power over their own actions, and so the Mu'tazila affirmed human free will. On this view attributing all human acts to God would render divine reward and punishment unjust. The principle also shaped Mu'tazili treatments of good and evil, obligation, and what God does for creatures. The Ash'ari school contested this framework. Where the Mu'tazila appealed to divine justice to include human beings within the circle of real agents, al-Ash'ari and his followers appealed to divine omnipotence to reserve real causal efficacy to God alone, developing the doctrine of kasb (acquisition), whereby God creates the act and its accompanying created power in the human, who thereby "acquires" it and bears responsibility. The two schools thus prioritized differently: the Mu'tazila foregrounded justice, the Ash'ariyya omnipotence. This debate is closely bound up with qadar (divine determination) and with the reconciliation of human responsibility and divine decree. The doctrine is also central to Twelver Shi'i theology, which counts ʿadl among its roots of religion.
editorial_notes: This entry frames ʿadl primarily as the Mu'tazili principle the prompt identifies, while noting that divine justice is also a foundational commitment in Twelver Shi'i theology. The Mu'tazila / Ash'ari contrast is presented as two attributed positions and is not adjudicated, per the neutrality policy; the associated Mu'tazili claim that God is obligated to do what is optimal (al-aslah) for creatures is a further, internally debated sub-doctrine deferred to a dedicated entry. Re-scoping pass (2026-07-18, peer-review follow-up): Frank's Metaphysics of Created Being is a narrow study of the early Muʿtazili Abû l-Hudhayl al-ʿAllâf (d. ca. 841) and cannot support the Muʿtazila-vs-Ashʿari kasb contrast (al-Ashʿari d. 936); it has therefore been re-scoped to carry only the early-Muʿtazili point that God's justice grounds human agency, and its confidence lowered from high to medium. The justice-vs-omnipotence / kasb contrast is now carried by Gimaret's Théories de l'acte humain en théologie musulmane (Vrin, 1980), the standard monograph on the topic (also used in qadar), at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line here. To triangulate with the tradition's own primary literature (neutrality principle #8), ʿAbd al-Jabbār's Sharḥ al-Uṣūl al-Khamsa, the classic Muʿtazilī exposition in which ʿadl is the second principle and which itself enumerates the five principles, is retained at medium confidence, as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line here. Earlier source-quality pass (neutrality principle #9): the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy entry and the WikiShia article were removed as a reference work and a tertiary wiki respectively. The entry now rests on two academic monographs (Frank, Gimaret) plus one classical primary text. A dedicated study of ʿAbd al-Jabbār and the al-uṣūl al-khamsa specifically would further strengthen the Western-academic coverage. Third-lens pass (2026-07-18): a modern/contemporary Muslim scholarly source has been added, Murtaḍā Muṭahharī's ʿAdl-i Ilāhī (Divine Justice), a twentieth-century Twelver Shiʿi monograph treating ʿadl directly (fitting since the entry already notes ʿadl as a root of religion in Twelver theology). The entry now triangulates three lenses: Western academic (Gimaret, Frank), the tradition's own classical primary text (ʿAbd al-Jabbār), and a modern Muslim scholar (Muṭahharī), which converge on the core of the doctrine, that a just God does no wrong and that this justice grounds real human agency and moral responsibility.

relations.note: ʿAdl is the second of the Mu'tazila's five principles and a defining commitment.
One of the five fundamental Mu'tazili principles.
Paired with tawhid as the two most emphasized Mu'tazili principles.
Divine justice grounds the Mu'tazili affirmation of free will against unqualified predestination.
The Ash'ari doctrine of acquisition, framed around divine omnipotence rather than justice.
Divine justice underwrites recompense in the hereafter.

historical_period.note: Central to Mu'tazili theology from its early development in Basra and Baghdad (8th century) through the classical debates with the Ash'ariyya (10th–11th centuries).

sources.citation: Frank, Richard M. The Metaphysics of Created Being According to Abû l-Hudhayl al-ʿAllâf: A Philosophical Study of the Earliest Kalâm. Istanbul: Nederlands Historisch-Archaeologisch Instituut in het Nabije Oosten, 1966. A scholarly monograph on the theology of the early Muʿtazili Abû l-Hudhayl al-ʿAllâf (d. ca. 841). Cited here only for the early-Muʿtazili point that God's justice (ʿadl) grounds the affirmation of human beings as real agents of their own acts; it is a narrow study of one early figure and does not carry the later Muʿtazila-vs-Ashʿari kasb contrast.

Gimaret, Daniel. Théories de l'acte humain en théologie musulmane. Paris: Librairie Philosophique J. Vrin; Louvain: Éditions Peeters, 1980. The standard monograph on Islamic theological theories of human action. Carries the justice-vs-omnipotence framing and the Muʿtazila-vs-Ashʿari contrast: the Muʿtazili doctrine that humans originate their own acts (grounded in divine justice) against the Ashʿari doctrine of kasb (acquisition), whereby God creates the act and its power while the human acquires it.

ʿAbd al-Jabbār al-Hamadhānī, al-Qāḍī. Sharḥ al-Uṣūl al-Khamsa [Commentary on the Five Principles]. Muʿtazilī kalām work (late 10th / early 11th c. CE). The classic exposition of the five principles, in which divine justice (ʿadl), that God does no wrong and cannot be the author of human injustice, so that humans must possess real power over their acts, is elaborated as the second principle.

Muṭahharī, Murtaḍā. ʿAdl-i Ilāhī [Divine Justice]. English trans. Sulayman Hasan Abidi and Murtaza Alidina, Divine Justice, Tehran: International Publishing Company / ICAS-affiliated editions, 2004. A modern Twelver Shiʿi scholarly monograph devoted entirely to divine justice (ʿadl), by the contemporary Iranian theologian and philosopher Murtaḍā Muṭahharī (d. 1979). Treats ʿadl as a foundational principle (a root of religion in Twelver theology), addressing the justice of God, human free will and responsibility, and the problem of evil and suffering. Added as the modern Muslim scholarly lens; cited at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line.
